identity.title: Research and Development in the Social Sciences and Humanities

lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing external social-science or humanities research, survey research, behavioral and economic research, program and policy evaluation, qualitative research, and related study design, data collection, analysis, and reporting.
lens.excluded_activities: Academic departments, captive research units, grant-only or self-directed institutes without a repeat outsourced-service model, advocacy or publishing operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include government agencies, foundations, nonprofits, and businesses. Revenue commonly comes from task orders, multiyear studies, cost-reimbursement contracts, time-based work, fixed-fee deliverables, and recurring evaluation or evidence-building programs.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS measures employment shares and mean wages by occupation within NAICS 541720, while AAPOR identifies AI-susceptible and human-critical tasks in survey research. Transformation: occupation employment and wage data are used to approximate wage weights, then tasks such as literature synthesis, questionnaire drafting, coding, analysis, and report production are mapped to exposed work. Direction of error: survey-research guidance may overstate exposure for field-heavy humanities work, while occupation titles conceal automatable subtasks and may understate exposure.
a.rationale: The industry is unusually knowledge-work intensive. BLS reports large shares in social-science, business, computer, research-assistant, and administrative occupations, and AAPOR documents credible AI use in instrument drafting, literature synthesis, coding, theme identification, and exploratory analysis. Research design, primary fieldwork, causal judgment, ethics, and client accountability remain less substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A task-level time study from a representative set of NAICS 541720 firms, linked to payroll by role and separating already-automated work, would materially refine the wage-weighted exposed share.
caveats: BLS occupation data are from May 2023 and omit the self-employed.
AAPOR focuses on survey research, which is important but not coextensive with social-science and humanities R&D.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a vintage mismatch and therefore may not represent the same operating year as the occupation evidence.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup measures current AI use and workflow effects across employed U.S. adults, and AAPOR describes implementation controls for survey research. Transformation: current knowledge-worker adoption and research-specific governance constraints are translated into the share of exposed opportunity that a lower-middle-market research operator could implement within five years. Direction of error: economy-wide adoption may understate uptake in digitally capable research firms, but self-reported productivity and tool use may overstate production-grade implementation.
rho.rationale: Adoption is already broad enough to support substantial implementation, especially in drafting, synthesis, coding, and analysis. Gallup nevertheless finds that workplace transformation remains limited, while AAPOR requires human review, validation, disclosure, privacy controls, and auditability. These constraints keep the base well below full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A structured survey of operating leaders at NAICS 541720 firms covering production deployment, review time, error rates, client restrictions, and realized labor hours by workflow would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Gallup covers the full U.S. employee population rather than this industry or firm-size band.
The five-year estimate assumes continued model improvement and workable data-governance controls without assuming that experimental use becomes production use automatically.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is centered on research and analysis, and NCSES shows meaningful externally funded business R&D, supporting a sizable outsourced-service population. Eligibility is reduced by grant-only institutes, self-directed research organizations, captive-like operations, and firms whose work is too episodic or principal-dependent to constitute a repeat transferable service.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census from business databases, federal award records, Form 990 filings, and company websites that tags revenue model, customer concentration, repeat work, independence, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from receipts and may misstate the denominator for nonprofit, grant-funded, or unusually labor-intensive firms.
Firm-level NAICS assignment can blur program evaluation, management consulting, market research, and social-science R&D.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. Transformation: that intention rate is discounted for plans that do not complete, family gifts and internal transfers that are not qualifying control events, and research firms lacking transferable operations. Direction of error: the broad small-business sample may overstate qualifying events by including nonmarket transfers, but may understate them because lower-middle-market firms are generally more sale-ready than very small employers.
s5.rationale: A meaningful pool of employer owners expects an ownership event, but stated plans are not completed deals and the qualifying definition is narrower. Founder reputation, principal-investigator relationships, and dependence on key technical staff further reduce the share of eligible research firms that can complete a transferable control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: Completed control-deal counts and denominators for social-research, survey-research, and program-evaluation firms from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and specialist broker interviews would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
Intentions may not result in transactions, and the survey measure includes transfers that may not satisfy the control-transfer definition.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: FAR Part 35 describes federal R&D contract form, stating that cost-reimbursement is usually appropriate when specifications and costs are uncertain, while fixed-price work can be used for defined minor projects. Transformation: this contract-form guidance is translated into a discounted retention share for a mixed customer book, with low retention on reimbursed work and higher retention on fixed-fee deliverables and growth capacity. Direction of error: the federal rule may understate retention where commercial and foundation customers buy fixed deliverables, but may overstate it if competitive renewals rapidly reprice labor savings across the broader market.
q.rationale: Cost-reimbursement and time-based arrangements pass labor savings to customers directly or through renewal pricing. Fixed-fee studies can retain savings during a contract term, and faster delivery can create capacity, but competitive procurement and transparent labor categories limit durable capture.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level diligence on billing form, fee structure, indirect-cost treatment, renewal cadence, price competition, change orders, and realized margin after automation at a representative set of firms would materially change retention.
caveats: FAR Part 35 governs federal R&D contracting and does not measure the industry's customer or contract mix.
Grant-funded work and reimbursable contracts may permit little direct margin capture, while fixed-fee private work may permit substantially more.
This range excludes demand loss and implementation friction, which are represented elsewhere.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: NCSES measures externally funded domestic business R&D in NAICS 541720, BLS projects employment for constituent social-science occupations, and GSA documents a federal channel for program-evaluation contractors. Transformation: sponsored R&D spending, occupation outlooks, and procurement infrastructure are combined into a constant-price year-five quantity ratio for the current service basket. Direction of error: nominal R&D spending overstates real quantity when prices rise, occupation growth can understate output when productivity improves, and the adjacent GSA evaluation category may overstate demand attributable to this NAICS code.
d5.rationale: NCSES reports only modest nominal growth in externally funded domestic industry R&D between 2021 and 2023, implying weak real growth. BLS outlooks are mixed across relevant occupations, while GSA's evaluation subgroup supports continuing evidence-building demand. Public-budget exposure and project timing justify a wide downside despite durable uses in health, education, policy, and business research.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar time series of NAICS 541720 receipts and awarded backlog, segmented by federal, state, foundation, nonprofit, and commercial customer, plus recompete and cancellation rates, would materially change the demand ratio.
caveats: NCSES business R&D expenditures cover only R&D-performing companies and are not total industry receipts.
The NCSES comparison uses 2021 and 2023 nominal dollars and may reflect survey revision, sampling error, or classification changes.
BLS occupation projections span the whole economy, and GSA's subgroup sits under a neighboring consulting category.
Government funding decisions can change faster than the historical evidence indicates.

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: AAPOR assesses whether AI can perform survey-research tasks and identifies human-oversight requirements, while the BLS occupation mix shows the breadth of expert and research-assistant labor in NAICS 541720. Transformation: task-level evidence is translated into the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable research operator. Direction of error: survey research may be more standardized and therefore more self-serviceable than field-heavy social research, but synthetic data and automated analysis may improve faster than current guidance anticipates and make this estimate too high.
o.rationale: Clients can increasingly self-serve literature synthesis, preliminary analysis, simple instruments, and report drafting. The operator remains important for study design, sampling, field execution, sensitive interviewing, causal interpretation, privacy, disclosure, validation, and defensible conclusions. AAPOR specifically warns that synthetic respondents are not a reliable substitute for human data and that autonomous interviewing creates material oversight risks.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and win-loss data showing which studies were canceled, internalized, replaced by software, or awarded to accountable external operators, paired with validation results for synthetic respondents and autonomous interviewing, would materially change this share.
caveats: AAPOR's guidance is survey-specific and reflects current technology rather than proven year-five capability.
Operator requirement varies sharply between simple desk research and regulated, longitudinal, sensitive, or field-based studies.
This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service, not internal labor efficiency.

narrative.executive_view: Social-science and humanities R&D combines high labor intensity with a large body of digitally mediated knowledge work. AI can materially compress production effort, but public procurement, research integrity, and founder dependence constrain both retained economics and transaction availability.
narrative.ai_impact: The clearest opportunities are literature synthesis, questionnaire drafting, open-text coding, theme extraction, exploratory analysis, documentation, and report production. Human researchers remain central to design, sampling, fieldwork, sensitive interviews, causal judgment, validation, and accountable sign-off.
narrative.value_capture: Retention depends heavily on contract form. Savings in cost-reimbursement or time-based work tend to pass through, whereas fixed-fee studies can preserve near-term margin and faster throughput can create capacity. Recompetes and transparent labor pricing erode that advantage over time.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry contains external contractors, but the eligible pool is diluted by grant-only institutes, self-directed organizations, and principal-dependent practices. Broad owner survey evidence supports some transfer activity, though qualifying completed control deals are likely less common than stated ownership plans.
narrative.demand_durability: Evaluation, evidence-building, behavioral research, and policy analysis have persistent uses, yet the available data point to muted recent real growth rather than a strong secular expansion. Dependence on government and foundation budgets creates meaningful cancellation and funding-cycle risk.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainties are the true contract mix, the share of firms with recurring outsourced revenue, future public funding, client rules for AI use, and whether quality review consumes expected labor savings. Synthetic respondents, privacy failures, biased coding, or weak audit trails could create reputational and contractual liability.
narrative.principal_driver: A wage-heavy mix of research, analytical, writing, and administrative tasks offers substantial implementable workflow automation.
narrative.principal_weakness: Cost-reimbursement pricing and public-budget exposure can prevent productivity gains from becoming durable operator cash flow.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition for Research and Development in the Social Sciences and Humanities
sources.supports: Defines the industry's research and analysis scope across social sciences and humanities.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 541720
sources.supports: Provides same-industry occupation employment shares and wages used to approximate wage-weighted task exposure and operator-required work.

sources.title: Responsible AI Integration in Survey Research
sources.supports: Identifies AI-susceptible research tasks, validation requirements, human oversight, privacy risks, interviewing limitations, and weaknesses of synthetic respondents.

sources.title: Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics
sources.supports: Shows mixed projected employment change across social scientists, survey researchers, research assistants, and related occupations.

sources.title: Business Enterprise Research and Development: 2023
sources.supports: Reports 2023 domestic R&D for NAICS 541720, including externally funded R&D and source-of-funds detail.

sources.title: Domestic R&D Paid for by Others and Performed by the Company, by Type of R&D, Industry, and Company Size: 2021
sources.supports: Provides the 2021 same-industry baseline for externally funded domestic business R&D.

sources.title: FAR Part 35: Research and Development Contracting
sources.supports: Explains use of contracts versus grants and why cost-reimbursement is usually appropriate for uncertain R&D, with fixed pricing available for sufficiently defined work.

sources.title: Program Evaluation Services Subgroup
sources.supports: Documents a federal procurement channel for pre-vetted contractors providing program evaluation and evidence-building services.

sources.title: Rising AI Adoption Spurs Workforce Changes
sources.supports: Measures U.S. employee AI adoption, reported productivity benefits, and the still-limited transformation of organizational workflows in 2026.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports five-year sale or ownership-transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners and documents the survey population.
